description: A high-level architecture diagram of an LLM-powered autonomous agent system, showing the LLM as the central brain connected to components: Planning (with sub-components like reflection and decomposition), Memory (sensory, short-term, long-term), and Tool Use (external APIs, databases, etc.).
teaching_value: Gives students a mental map of the entire agent system before diving into individual components, making it easier to contextualize each subsystem (planning, memory, tools) as part of a coherent whole.
when_to_show: Show at the very start of any lesson on LLM agents to establish the overall framework; return to it as a reference when introducing each individual component.
suggested_caption: LLM-powered autonomous agent system overview with planning, memory, and tools. (Weng, 2023)

description: Side-by-side comparison of ReAct reasoning trajectories for knowledge-intensive tasks (HotpotQA, FEVER) and decision-making tasks (AlfWorld, WebShop), showing interleaved Thought, Action, and Observation steps versus Act-only baselines.
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates how ReAct interleaves reasoning traces with actions, helping students see the practical difference between pure action-taking and thought-augmented acting across two distinct task types.
when_to_show: Introduce when explaining the ReAct framework; use the side-by-side format to contrast reasoning-augmented vs. Act-only approaches and discuss why Thought steps improve performance.
suggested_caption: ReAct reasoning trajectories for knowledge and decision tasks. (Yao et al., 2023)

description: A flowchart illustrating the Reflexion framework, showing the cycle between Actor (LLM), Evaluator, Self-Reflection model, and Memory buffer, with trajectories feeding back as verbal reinforcement signals for iterative improvement.
teaching_value: Visualizes how self-reflection creates a feedback loop that substitutes for gradient-based learning, helping students understand how verbal self-critique stored in memory enables iterative agent improvement without weight updates.
when_to_show: Show when introducing Reflexion after ReAct; use the cycle diagram to explain how the memory buffer accumulates reflections and how this differs from standard RL reward signals.
suggested_caption: Reflexion framework: actor, evaluator, and self-reflection feedback loop. (Shinn & Labash, 2023)

description: Bar charts comparing Reflexion against baselines on AlfWorld and HotpotQA benchmarks, broken down by failure type (hallucination vs. inefficient planning), showing performance across multiple trial iterations.
teaching_value: Provides empirical evidence for Reflexion's effectiveness and reveals that hallucination is the dominant failure mode in AlfWorld, teaching students to distinguish between types of agent failures and how iterative reflection addresses them.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the Reflexion framework to ground the theory in experimental results; prompt students to analyze why hallucination dominates over inefficient planning as a failure mode.
suggested_caption: Reflexion results on AlfWorld and HotpotQA; hallucination dominates failures. (Shinn & Labash, 2023)

description: A diagram showing the Chain of Hindsight (CoH) fine-tuning process, where a model receives a sequence of past outputs annotated with feedback ranked by quality, and learns to produce incrementally better outputs conditioned on that history.
teaching_value: Illustrates how presenting ranked feedback sequences during fine-tuning teaches a model to self-improve, helping students understand the difference between standard supervised fine-tuning and feedback-conditioned learning.
when_to_show: Introduce when covering self-reflection mechanisms beyond prompting; contrast with Reflexion to show a training-time vs. inference-time approach to agent self-improvement.
suggested_caption: Chain of Hindsight fine-tuning produces incrementally improved outputs from feedback. (Liu et al., 2023)

description: A diagram illustrating Algorithm Distillation (AD), showing how multi-episode learning histories from source RL policies are used to train a neural network via behavioral cloning, enabling in-context reinforcement learning.
teaching_value: Explains the non-obvious idea that a learning algorithm itself can be distilled into a neural network by training on histories of improving behavior, helping students understand in-context RL as an emergent capability.
when_to_show: Use when discussing advanced self-improvement mechanisms; contrast with CoH and Reflexion to show a third paradigm where the improvement process itself is learned from historical trajectories.
suggested_caption: Algorithm Distillation distills RL learning histories into a neural network. (Laskin et al., 2023)

description: Performance comparison charts of AD, Expert Distillation (ED), source policy, and RL² across memory and exploration environments (dark environments with A3C, watermaze with DQN), measuring learning efficiency with binary rewards.
teaching_value: Demonstrates empirically that AD achieves near-optimal in-context RL performance, and shows students how multi-episode context length affects learning, reinforcing the theoretical claim that longer context enables better in-context RL.
when_to_show: Show after explaining AD to validate the approach empirically; use to discuss the role of context window length in enabling in-context RL and the trade-offs between different distillation methods.
suggested_caption: AD vs. ED, source policy, and RL² on memory/exploration tasks with binary reward. (Laskin et al., 2023)

description: A hierarchical categorization diagram of human memory types: sensory memory (iconic, echoic, haptic), short-term/working memory, and long-term memory (explicit/declarative and implicit/procedural), mapped to their LLM agent analogues.
teaching_value: Bridges cognitive science and AI by mapping human memory taxonomy to LLM agent memory components, helping students understand design choices for agent memory systems through a familiar conceptual framework.
when_to_show: Show at the start of the memory component section; use the human analogy to motivate why agent memory is structured the way it is before introducing technical implementations like vector stores.
suggested_caption: Human memory taxonomy mapped to LLM agent memory components. (Weng, 2023)

description: A recall@10 performance comparison chart of multiple approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) / MIPS algorithms (e.g., HNSW, FAISS, ScaNN) plotted against query speed, showing the accuracy-speed trade-off.
teaching_value: Helps students understand the practical engineering trade-off between retrieval accuracy and speed when choosing a vector search algorithm for agent external memory, grounding the abstract concept of MIPS in concrete benchmarks.
when_to_show: Introduce when discussing external memory implementation; use to explain why approximate methods are preferred over exact search and how to evaluate the accuracy-latency trade-off in production systems.
suggested_caption: MIPS algorithm comparison: recall@10 vs. query speed trade-off. (Google Blog, 2020)

description: A multi-stage pipeline diagram showing HuggingGPT's architecture: user request flows through task planning, model selection, task execution, and response generation stages, with the LLM acting as a central controller coordinating specialized AI models from HuggingFace.
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates how an LLM can orchestrate multiple specialized models as tools, making abstract agent architecture tangible by showing task decomposition, dependency tracking, and multi-model coordination in a single visual flow.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the 'Tool Use' component of LLM agents, specifically to demonstrate how a real system (HuggingGPT) chains task planning → model selection → execution → response, reinforcing the four-stage pipeline described in the text.
suggested_caption: HuggingGPT's four-stage pipeline: LLM plans, selects, and coordinates AI models. (Shen et al. 2023)

description: Pseudo-code block illustrating the decision logic an LLM follows when making API calls in the API-Bank benchmark, showing conditional branching for API selection, parameter filling, and call execution with evaluation checkpoints at each step.
teaching_value: Makes explicit the multi-step decision process hidden inside tool-use: the LLM must decide whether to call an API, which API to call, and how to fill parameters — each a separately evaluable skill, helping students understand granular agent evaluation.
when_to_show: Surface this after explaining HuggingGPT to deepen understanding of how tool-use accuracy is measured; use it when discussing how benchmarks decompose agent capability into discrete, testable decision points.
suggested_caption: API-Bank pseudo-code showing LLM decision logic for API selection and execution. (Li et al. 2023)

description: Architecture diagram of the Generative Agents system showing how a single LLM-powered agent processes observations through a memory stream, retrieves relevant memories via reflection, and uses a planning module to produce actions in a simulated social environment with 25 virtual characters.
teaching_value: Illustrates how memory, reflection, and planning modules combine to produce believable long-term agent behavior, giving students a concrete architecture to contrast with simpler single-step LLM agents and understand emergent social simulation.
when_to_show: Introduce when transitioning from tool-use to memory and planning components; use it to anchor discussion of how persistent memory and self-reflection enable complex, human-like agent behavior over extended time horizons.
suggested_caption: Generative agent architecture: memory stream, reflection, and planning enable simulated human behavior. (Park et al. 2023)
